strategy_update: Implement graph coloring on the exam-conflict graph: (1) Build conflict edges from student overlap data; (2) Order exams by conflict degree (most constrained first: E1/E2 with 4 conflicts each); (3) Greedily assign each exam to the earliest period with zero conflicts to exams already in that period; (4) Distribute assigned exams across available rooms in a balanced manner. This ensures no two conflicting exams occupy the same period while respecting the limited period availability.

strategy_update: Apply graph coloring strategy: (1) Treat student_conflict as hard constraints - separate all conflicting pairs into different periods; (2) Map conflict graph to color classes, using 6-8 periods to ensure no two conflicting exams share a period; (3) Respect precedence constraint E5 before E6 and special availability for E13 (exclude period 4); (4) Assign rooms by capacity and availability; (5) Accept that soft constraint C1 cannot be satisfied due to hard conflict with E1↔E2.

strategy_update: 1. Enforce C1 first: assign E1 and E2 to the same period (period 1). 2. Build a conflict graph from student-sharing pairs and use greedy period assignment to separate conflicting exams into non-adjacent periods, prioritizing high-conflict exams (E1, E3, E11, E2, E4, E7, E8, E13). 3. Distribute remaining exams (E6, E9, E10, E14, E15, E16, E18) into periods without introducing new conflicts.

strategy_update: Build explicit student-overlap conflict graph before assignment. Identify conflict cliques (complete subgraphs requiring ≥3 periods) and assign each member to a different period. Process highest-degree conflict nodes first (E1, E2, E9, E5, E7, E8, E3). Verify no two conflicting exams share the same period before finalizing. Reserve periods for clique separation before placing low-conflict exams.

strategy_update: 1) Build complete conflict graph: identify all exam pairs that share ≥1 student (cannot coexist in same period). 2) Treat as graph coloring: assign exams to periods such that conflicting pairs get different periods. 3) Enforce hard precedence constraint E5→E6 (E5 before E6). 4) Enforce hard different-period constraint E3≠E4. 5) Assign room to each exam respecting availability and capacity. 6) Note: C1 (E1, E2 same period) conflicts with the student-conflict hard constraint, so it must be violated.

strategy_update: Build an explicit student conflict graph from enrollment data before period assignment. Treat this as a graph k-coloring problem where each exam is a node, edges connect exams with shared students, and colors represent periods. Ensure no two adjacent nodes (conflicting exams) receive the same color. Assign rooms only after valid period separation is confirmed.

strategy_update: Use a conflict-aware round-robin assignment: (1) Group exams with the highest degree of conflicts. (2) Distribute each group across non-consecutive periods to maximize separation. (3) Respect distribution constraints (C1: E10-E20 same period, C2: E5 before E6, C3: E3-E4 different). (4) Verify no two exams sharing ≥1 student occupy the same period. (5) Assign rooms matching capacity and availability.

strategy_update: Build a conflict graph from student-sharing pairs. Use graph coloring to assign periods: separate each conflicting pair to different periods. E9 (conflicts with E4 and E15) requires special attention—assign to period 3, distinct from both E4 (period 1) and E15 (period 4). Apply greedy assignment: place connected exams in different periods, reusing periods 1–4 to keep the schedule compact.
